segments.text: Now we move from the flight toward the landing.
For the landing, it's important that we've looked beforehand at what landing pattern we want to fly.
Is it a right-hand or a left-hand pattern?
And above all, it's important that we all follow the same rules.
Because in this phase of the flight we're no longer far from the ground.
That means we have no more time, or very little time, to react.
When we have 1000 metres above the ground, if someone doesn't do something the way they should,
we have relatively long to react and assess the situation.
When landing, everything happens a bit faster, simply because the ground is close.
So the principle has to be that we all stick to the landing pattern,
and so we can guarantee that we see what the other pilot, the one ahead, is doing.
The landing pattern looks like this: we decide on a left-hand or right-hand pattern
and then we come into the position circles, our altitude-loss circles,
where we bleed off our altitude.
To start a clean landing pattern, it's important that we circle in the same direction
in which we're going to do the landing pattern.
If we do a right-hand pattern, it's a right-hand circle.
If we do a left-hand pattern, it's a left-hand circle.
Then we go to an altitude — the landing pattern — that's healthy.
And that healthy altitude is something you learn to gauge after one, two, three landings.
I already have it under pretty good control.
By looking at the visual reference point each time, you notice,
ah, right now I'm still this high.
And as a rule, it's better to come in too high and still land properly
than too low, because altitude gives you time.
When you fly off from the position circle into the downwind leg,
for example, I'm always at tree height or I'm always at this house-level height
when I fly the downwind leg.
That can vary, depending on the air mass you're in;
it doesn't have to be the same at all.
It also changes completely when there's wind in play.
That's why it's important to develop a feel here.
You'll dare to do it; everyone has after two, three, four flights.
But that's why you're with the instructor on the radio,
where I can give you nice instructions,
so that at first you do it a bit remote-controlled
and after a certain number of flights you can do it on your own.
If we notice on the downwind that we're clearly too high,
we can simply turn onto base
and we can also extend the base leg.
If we have space, we go far, far forward and make a curve.
If we're too short, we notice we're not always that much higher.
Then we can also shorten the whole thing, as shown here.
Or we can also repeat the base leg with S-turns several times.
And the S-turns always have to follow proper reference points.
And the good thing about these S-turns is that at any moment we can
leave the base leg and turn onto final, if we see it fits.
We can of course make a slight correction
if the wind comes from a bit of an angle.
But ultimately the important thing is that we fly straight toward the landing point.
If we notice we're way too high, there are various options.
We can, for example, make ourselves big.
That is, the pilot below the wing creates a lot of drag.
And whether we sit compactly — then we glide well and have little drag.
That means we sit up out of the harness,
make sure that our landing gear, namely the legs, are already ready.
And then we create a lot of drag and have a poor glide.
That means we can bleed off altitude efficiently.
And that's how we approach the landing point.
Another option would be to fly very gentle S-turns.
Or, when we get to it later, to land with little-ears.
We pull the little-ears, the paraglider simply becomes smaller,
And that way you also have a better chance
At the briefing, by the way, it's a 30-metre circle
that you have to hit, inside the circle.
And then you've passed the landing nicely.
One more important thing: when you pull the little-ears,
you have to be aware that your arms are in such a position
that you can't steer much anymore.
You can steer with weight shift,
and you have to keep that in mind.
So better to land a bit further out
than do something rashly or under stress.
